title: The Havana Protocol
author: Alma Voss
blurb: Havana, 1961. A Soviet double agent. A CIA handler. A city on the edge of history. Marta Sokolova has one mission and one rule: never feel anything. When she meets Daniel Reyes on the Malecón, neither is who they claim to be. The word 'love' is never spoken. But everything depends on it.

title: The Vienna Cipher
author: Alma Voss
blurb: Vienna, August 12–14, 1961. The Berlin Wall goes up. Marta Sokolova and James Calloway — a burned-out MI6 handler with nothing left to lose — have 72 hours. Colder than The Havana Protocol. More cerebral. Set before it. Three days that change everything.

title: The Island That Swallowed Its Children
author: The Velatium
blurb: Castro, Cuba, and the price of a revolution that never ended. Before 1959, Cuba ranked second in Latin America by per capita income — more doctors per capita than France. What Castro inherited was not a failed state. What he built was one.

es_edition.title: La Isla Que Devoró a Sus Hijos

title: Before the Veil Came Down
author: The Velatium
blurb: Jina Mahsa Amini had two names: one for her family, one a state that banned Kurdish names would allow. Three days after Iran's morality police detained her over a hijab, she was dead — and a movement the regime couldn't kill was born. From the 1979 revolution to the assassination of Ali Khamenei, this is the fifty-year architecture of control, told through the women who knew the truth long before the world did.

title: What the Revolution Left Behind
author: The Velatium
blurb: In 2000, Venezuela sat atop the largest oil reserves on Earth — the wealthiest economy in South America. A generation later, a government had done what no war or earthquake could: erased three-quarters of it, and driven nearly a quarter of the population to flee on foot. The revolution kept its promise first. That's what makes what came after a betrayal, not just a failure.

title: The Country That Cannot Be Seen
author: The Velatium
blurb: At night, North Korea is a black void beside a blazing South Korea — 25 million people in a darkness so complete their country looks like ocean from space. Built from defector testimony, leaked laws, and the holes the dead leave in a census, this is the machinery that produces both the suffering and the six-decade endurance of the world's most closed society.
